title: Cholesterol Ratio Calculator
tagline: Calculate Total/HDL and LDL/HDL cholesterol ratios to assess your cardiovascular risk with clinical precision.
intro: Enter your lipid panel values to calculate key cholesterol ratios and receive an evidence-based cardiovascular risk assessment.
formula: Total/HDL Ratio = Total ÷ HDL  •  LDL/HDL Ratio = LDL ÷ HDL  •  Non-HDL = Total − HDL

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive values for Total Cholesterol and HDL Cholesterol.

about.heading: About the cholesterol ratio calculator
about.body:
Cholesterol ratios are among the most clinically meaningful tools available for assessing cardiovascular risk. Unlike individual cholesterol measurements, ratios capture the balance between protective HDL cholesterol and harmful atherogenic particles, providing a more nuanced picture of heart disease risk than any single number can offer. Research from landmark studies including the Framingham Heart Study has consistently demonstrated that the Total/HDL ratio and the LDL/HDL ratio are stronger predictors of cardiovascular events than total cholesterol alone.

The Total/HDL Ratio is calculated by dividing your total cholesterol by your HDL (high-density lipoprotein) cholesterol. HDL is often called "good cholesterol" because it acts as a scavenger, transporting excess cholesterol from arterial walls back to the liver for elimination. A lower Total/HDL ratio indicates that a greater proportion of your total cholesterol is the protective HDL type. Clinical guidelines generally consider a ratio below 3.5 as optimal, 3.5 to 5.0 as borderline, and above 5.0 as elevated risk requiring attention.

The LDL/HDL Ratio, when LDL data is available, provides additional precision. LDL (low-density lipoprotein) is the primary carrier of cholesterol into arterial walls, where it can form plaques that narrow blood vessels and trigger heart attacks and strokes. An LDL/HDL ratio below 2.0 is considered optimal, 2.0 to 3.0 is borderline, and above 3.0 indicates elevated cardiovascular risk. This ratio helps clinicians decide whether lifestyle modifications alone are sufficient or whether pharmacological intervention such as statin therapy is warranted.

Non-HDL cholesterol represents all atherogenic cholesterol particles and is calculated by subtracting HDL from total cholesterol. It captures LDL as well as VLDL and IDL, which also contribute to arterial plaque formation. Many cardiologists view non-HDL as a more complete risk marker than LDL alone, particularly in patients with elevated triglycerides. Targets are generally below 130 mg/dL for low-risk individuals and lower for those with established cardiovascular disease.

Important context for interpreting results: these ratios are statistical risk indicators derived from population studies and should always be evaluated alongside your complete clinical picture. Individual factors including age, sex, family history, blood pressure, smoking status, diabetes, and existing cardiovascular conditions all influence how your ratios translate into personal risk. A borderline ratio in a young, otherwise healthy individual carries very different implications from the same ratio in a patient with diabetes and hypertension. Always discuss your lipid panel results with a qualified healthcare provider who can integrate all relevant information into a personalized assessment and, if necessary, a treatment plan.

examples.heading: Cholesterol ratio examples
examples.intro: Load any example to see how the calculator handles different lipid profiles.

items.note: Optimal ratios — low cardiovascular risk, excellent HDL levels.
High-risk profile — both ratios exceed targets; lifestyle or medical intervention recommended.
Borderline profile — monitoring and lifestyle modifications advisable.
Excellent ratios without LDL — very low predicted cardiovascular risk.

howto.heading: How to use the cholesterol ratio calculator
steps: Obtain a recent fasting lipid panel from your doctor or lab; you will need Total Cholesterol and HDL Cholesterol at minimum.
Enter your Total Cholesterol (mg/dL) in the first field and HDL Cholesterol in the second field.
Optionally enter LDL Cholesterol and Triglycerides for a more complete risk picture.
Click Calculate Ratios to see your Total/HDL ratio, Non-HDL cholesterol, and (if provided) your LDL/HDL ratio with risk classifications.
Compare your results to the clinical thresholds shown and discuss any borderline or high-risk values with your healthcare provider.

items.q: What is a good Total/HDL cholesterol ratio?
items.a: A Total/HDL ratio below 3.5 is generally considered optimal, reflecting a healthy balance between total and protective HDL cholesterol. Ratios between 3.5 and 5.0 are borderline and may warrant lifestyle changes, while ratios above 5.0 indicate elevated cardiovascular risk that often merits medical evaluation.

items.q: Why is the cholesterol ratio considered better than total cholesterol alone?
items.a: Total cholesterol doesn't distinguish between protective HDL and harmful LDL/VLDL particles. A person with high total cholesterol due to very high HDL may actually have low cardiovascular risk, while someone with average total cholesterol but low HDL can be at elevated risk. Ratios capture this balance and have been shown in multiple population studies to be stronger predictors of heart disease than total cholesterol by itself.

Non-HDL cholesterol is total cholesterol minus HDL, and it encompasses all atherogenic (plaque-forming) lipoprotein particles including LDL, VLDL, and IDL. It is considered an increasingly reliable cardiovascular risk marker, especially in patients with high triglycerides where LDL calculations may be less accurate. The target is generally below 130 mg/dL for low-to-moderate risk individuals.

items.q: How often should I check my cholesterol ratios?
items.a: Most guidelines recommend a fasting lipid panel every 4 to 6 years for healthy adults starting at age 20, with more frequent testing (annually or every 1–2 years) for those with elevated risk, known cardiovascular disease, diabetes, or those on cholesterol-lowering medication. Your doctor will advise a schedule suited to your individual risk profile.

items.q: Can diet and exercise really improve cholesterol ratios?
items.a: Yes. A diet rich in soluble fiber, omega-3 fatty acids, and unsaturated fats while limiting saturated fat, trans fat, and refined carbohydrates can improve LDL and HDL levels meaningfully. Regular aerobic exercise is particularly effective at raising HDL. Together, lifestyle changes can improve cholesterol ratios by 10–20%, which may be sufficient for borderline-risk individuals without requiring medication.

items.q: Is this calculator a substitute for medical advice?
items.a: No. The ratios and risk labels are educational tools based on population-level guidelines and do not account for your complete health picture. Factors such as age, sex, blood pressure, diabetes, smoking, and family history all influence cardiovascular risk interpretation. Always discuss your lipid panel and these results with a qualified healthcare professional before making treatment decisions.
